item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: The image that was used in this article is non-compliant with the SCP wiki's license (CC BY-SA 3.0) as no source found indicates it has been released under Creative Commons. Thus, the image has been removed.
Congratulations.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Fixed a "through" that was supposed to be "though" (I can do that, right? I'm a bit of a noob). Also…

…living or non-living, have been destroyed.

Living OR non-living? So it only does one? I think you mean living and non-living, because otherwise you are implying that the flame chooses "Hmmm, do I want to engulf a person today? Or am I feeling more like consuming material goods?"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm surprised that no one else had test 3 strike their feels, as it stroke mine. Now if you excuse me, I'ma cri evrytiem in my head ;-;

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Hm. Certainly well-written, but the concept and execution don't do much for me. No vote.

item.title: I like it.
item.content:encoded:
I like the premise behind this and it is very clear you put quite amount of thought into the writing, which is also very good. For being your first SCP, I'm very impressed. It's better than my first was, but then again my first one was terrible.

Anyway, good job on a nice SCP.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I know why they didn't test with D-class, because D class live in dormitories on-site. Given the Foundations implied prior knowledge of the effects, they would expect a D-class test subject to cause a fire that could well destroy the entire facility.

In that vein, I thought that test 3 would result in the robotic equipment storage area bursting into flame.

What would happen if someone used a second match? Not at the same time, the tests cover that, but after their first match/home is burnt out.

Or how about "rootless" types? People who, either because they are always moving or have emotional issues, don't really have a place they consider "home". It could be as simple as someone who has recently been displaced (a natural house fire, maybe) and hasn't even made arrangemetns for where they will sleep tonight.

Feel free to ignore all that. This just makes me want to know more about how it works. And provoking curiosity is a sure sign of good writing. Well-done.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I am so happy this one is doing well, it has been added to my "SCP" bookmark folder on my computer. Love it

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: "Some men just want to watch the world burn" takes on a whole new meaning :/

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I just figured out what bothered me. How on earth would the Foundation know what the matches did before initially using them? I can see maybe recruiting some non-D's for more controlled experiments once it's been identified, but not before then.

Wow, how many D-Class would've been subjected to the effects before they figured out what was going on? I imagine it'd take the first one to think of his old penitentiary as home before they made the connection then started backtracking the effect.

Edit:
Just realized I should have made this in response to Reject's post down below

Well, it would be direct assuming the Foundation knows its true nature. Feel free to shoot me a PM if you want and I'll be glad to help you. This is very good for a first SCP, though!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Is it bad that my brain has already come up with a way that loss of containment on this SCP could lead to a world-ending disaster? (Mainly, by falling into the hands of someone with either no home, or who considers 'the world' his home-a person that's comfortably everywhere…)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
While they would have more control using an agent or researcher as a control, the problem would be unknown side effects that could affect the Foundation or its personnel negatively.

SCP-884 is the SCP to turn to for why the Foundation should be wary of anything they think they understand enough to let people use and why the O5 are banned from most SCP usage.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well, it would be direct assuming the Foundation knows its true nature. Feel free to shoot me a PM if you want and I'll be glad to help you. This is very good for a first SCP, though!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks; I'm hoping that I'll be able to get a better grasp on tone/execution as I get more involved with the site. Glad I can come up with some solid ideas though! Anyway, I like the idea of making things more subtle, but I feel like a document outlining an SCP and its effects would be direct as possible.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Veeery nice concept. The execution could use some work, but it's good enough to upvote. Personally, I'd suggest making the place that it lights on fire slightly more subtle instead of outright saying it in order to give the reader that ZOMG moment. Anyways, +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Responding to the questions/concerns in order, but thanking you in advance for bringing these issues up!
1. I tried to imply as much as I can that the matches are reusable, but you're right; that should probably be explicitly stated. Adding it in on my next edit.
2. Nope! The only way to light one of the matches is to strike it against SCP-1596. They don't work otherwise.
3. It was my idea that the flames on the matches are "sterile"; i.e.. they won't/can't spread off the match onto another substance. Somehow this didn't make it into the final edit though. Thanks for reminding me about it!

EDIT: Added the descriptions mentioned in Answers 1 and 2 to the second paragraph of the description, as well as throwing in some additional experiments to the first two test logs. Thanks again for calling these questions to my attention!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Despite being lit, matches from SCP-1596 will exhibit no signs of damage after their flames extinguish

You might want to explicitly state that a single match can be reused endlessly.

attempts to light the matches on any object other than SCP-1596 have proven futile.

So if you stick one of the matches in an open flame it won't burn?

Also, what happens if a match is used to light a candle? Will the candle burn normally, or go out when the match does? What if you try to use the match to light a house on fire?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Fun idea. I would have liked more experiments, but that's no reason to withhold an upvote. Good work.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The thought was that the Foundation would have more control over the parameters of the the experiments if the Subject was involved. If you used D-Class Personnel, you'd have to track down their homes and either evacuate them or allow whoever or whatever's inside to burn with the building, both of which would attract a lot of attention and, in the latter case, might be a tad too morally questionable for routine experimentation. It would be easier and more feasible to have the Subject on board as a willing participant who would collaborate with and keep quiet about the testing.

As for the side effect, we're assuming the Foundation already knows the properties of 1596 and is just testing the parameters (distance, whether or not direct contact is needed, etc.) of its effect.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: For a first try, this is pretty darned good. Welcome to the club.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This isn't bad, but why on earth are they testing this on agents? There's a reason we use D-class; we never know when there's some awful side effect we've missed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Glad you like it! I tried to get nail the tone down, but admittedly it did come out a little rough. I might tinker around with it some in the sandbox just to sand off the edges and make it all more consistent with the rest of the site. Thanks again!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Update: Took out the origin. Re-reading the entry, it didn't really contribute much to the article, and created too many plot holes. Reader will just have to leave 1596's discovery up to their imaginations for now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: A very interesting idea, with decent execution. For a first SCP, this is pretty darn good. It has some tone issues, but they're minor ones.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You did a great job of just that. Bravo.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I made a point of trying to have as few tests as possible while still establishing the effects and theories of SCP-1596. I realized that constantly over-paying for houses that were being burnt down would be a pretty big waste of resources, so I tired to keep it all as concise as possible and make sure that each one had a clear goal.

Glad you enjoyed it and thanks for the upvote!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I actually liked this a lot. I think it is a cool idea and was executed well.
Also, I felt that each test had meaning, testing distance and human contact.

I like. 1+

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Sorry about that; Thank you!

Edit: Responding to your questions now. For the testing, I was trying to imply that every one of the subjects was hand-picked by the foundation and informed of SCP-1596's properties beforehand, and not just randomly selected and tested blindly. Admittedly, if any of the agents felt most "at home" at the Foundation, that could cause a problem. But the researchers weren't aware of that until after Test 3, and all testing was suspended immediately afterwards.

As for the recovery…yeah, admittedly, if there's any weak spot to the story, it'd be that. I actually considered leaving the recovery story out entirely for that reason, but thought I'd see what the reaction to it was first. I'm willing to ditch that aspect if need be, or at least heavily edit it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Boa, you so nice. Be my friend?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Fixed your title for you.

Alright, read through most of it, it's 'okay', the tone seems fine (I'm a little tipsy, excuse me if I'm wildly mistaken) and I like the idea. However I must question it's origin and how the Foundation even discovered what it does, improbable things survive fires all the time (no really, it's not that weird), second how did they even become alerted to this and jump to the conclusion that it's anomalous and burns down houses? lastly how did they even find out it burned down houses?

I also must question the Foundation testing anything at all once they found out what it does, just imagine if some poor ass who lived on-site at a Keter facility lit one of these matches "there goes all of the world destroying and super hard to contain monsters, whoops!"

This is one of those cases where I like the idea, the execution is okay, but the finer details on origin mess up my enjoyment.

item.title: Authors Note
item.content:encoded: My first SCP; been working it out in the Sandbox for a while, so I’m hoping I managed to smooth out most of the kinks. But anyway, let me know what you all think; I think it’s a neat idea, but I’m afraid others might find it a little too bland. Thanks in advance for reading and critiquing!
